sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 30-year-old male who presented with a 2-week history of right knee pain following a fall from a tandem bike.
He reports that he hit his knee on the curb and took some ibuprofen, but the pain persists.
He has tried taking ibuprofen for more than 2 weeks, which he acknowledges is not recommended.
He denies any pre-existing knee problems and states that his knee was normal before the incident.
Past medical history: The patient works in a children's hospital as a baby rocker and spends most of his free time walking his dog.
He does not engage in any athletic activities other than the occasional swim.
Review of systems: The patient reports that his knee hurts when walking up hills, but he denies any weakness in the knee.
Current medications: The patient has been taking ibuprofen as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's right knee shows medial joint line tenderness, a dime-sized abrasion over the medial patella, and a little laxity in the posterior drawer.
The left knee is normal.
The patient has full extension and flexion in both knees, with no deformities or crepitus.
Test Results: The patient had x-rays taken, which showed no obvious abnormalities, but the doctor suspects a possible injury to the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL).
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a PCL injury and possibly other internal knee damage.
Problem list: PCL injury; possible meniscus tear or cartilage damage; knee instability.
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen up to 3 times a day for pain management, and I will prescribe a different medication that may be easier to take and less frequent.
I will also refer the patient to physical therapy and order a brace for temporary support.
I will schedule a follow-up appointment in 4-6 weeks to reassess the patient's progress.
Follow-up: The patient will return in 4-6 weeks for a follow-up appointment.
